episodes.english_context: Passage from Ramayana describing Rama's lineage. 'Raja-putrah' means 'son of the king'.
episodes.decision_prompt: What type of samasa (compound) is 'raja-putrah' (son of the king)?
candidate_options: Tatpurusha (determinative) — 'son of the king', second member is head, first qualifies it via case relation
Dvandva (copulative) — 'king and son', both members equally prominent, joined by 'and'
Bahuvrihi (possessive) — 'he who has a king as son', compound describes an outside referent
Avyayibhava (adverbial) — compound functions as an indeclinable adverb modifying the action
episodes.correct_answer: Tatpurusha (determinative) — 'son of the king', second member is head, first qualifies it via case relation
episodes.explanation: Raja-putrah is a shashthi-tatpurusha: 'son (putrah) of the king (rajnah)'. The second member 'putra' is the semantic head. The first member 'raja' stands in a genitive (shashthi) relationship to it.

episodes.english_context: Passage from Kalidasa's Meghaduta describing a blue lotus blooming in the lake.
episodes.decision_prompt: What type of samasa is 'nila-utpalam' (blue lotus)?
candidate_options: Tatpurusha (determinative) — 'lotus of blueness', second member is head qualified by first via case
Karmadharaya (descriptive tatpurusha) — 'blue lotus', adjective + noun where both refer to the same thing
Dvandva (copulative) — 'blue and lotus', two equal coordinate members
Bahuvrihi (possessive) — 'that which has a blue lotus', describes an external referent
episodes.correct_answer: Karmadharaya (descriptive tatpurusha) — 'blue lotus', adjective + noun where both refer to the same thing
episodes.explanation: Nila-utpalam is a karmadharaya compound: an adjective (nila, blue) modifying a noun (utpala, lotus) where both refer to the same entity. Karmadharaya is a subtype of tatpurusha with sama-adhikarana (same referent) between members.

episodes.english_context: Ramayana, Aranya Kanda. Rama and Lakshmana both entered the forest together.
episodes.decision_prompt: What type of samasa is 'rama-lakshmanau' (Rama and Lakshmana)?
candidate_options: Tatpurusha (determinative) — 'Lakshmana of Rama', second member qualified by first
Bahuvrihi (possessive) — 'he who has Rama and Lakshmana', describes an outside person
Dvandva (copulative) — 'Rama and Lakshmana', both members equally prominent, dual number reflects two referents
Avyayibhava (adverbial) — compound functions as an indeclinable adverb
episodes.correct_answer: Dvandva (copulative) — 'Rama and Lakshmana', both members equally prominent, dual number reflects two referents
episodes.explanation: Rama-lakshmanau is a dvandva compound. Both members (Rama, Lakshmana) are semantically coordinate — 'Rama AND Lakshmana'. The dual ending (-au) confirms two equal referents. Dvandva always takes the number of the sum of its members.

episodes.english_context: Arthashastra passage about a father and son staying at home to be examined by the minister.
episodes.decision_prompt: What type of samasa is 'pita-putrau' (father and son)?
candidate_options: Dvandva (copulative) — 'father and son', both members coordinate, dual ending confirms two referents
Tatpurusha (determinative) — 'son of the father', second member is head
Bahuvrihi (possessive) — 'he who has a father-son pair', refers to an external entity
Karmadharaya (descriptive) — adjective-noun pair referring to the same entity
episodes.correct_answer: Dvandva (copulative) — 'father and son', both members coordinate, dual ending confirms two referents
episodes.explanation: Pita-putrau is a dvandva: 'father (pita) AND son (putra)'. The dual ending (-au) signals two distinct coordinate referents. Neither member is subordinate to the other semantically.

episodes.english_context: Upanishad passage. 'Pitamahah' refers to Brahma, the grandfatherly creator who is called omniscient.
episodes.decision_prompt: What type of samasa is 'pitamahah' (grandfather / Brahma)?
candidate_options: Dvandva (copulative) — 'father and great', two coordinate members
Avyayibhava (adverbial) — compound used as indeclinable adverb
Tatpurusha (determinative) — 'great father' or 'father's father', second member is head modified by first
Bahuvrihi (possessive) — 'he for whom the father is great', describes the referent via an attribute
episodes.correct_answer: Tatpurusha (determinative) — 'great father' or 'father's father', second member is head modified by first
episodes.explanation: Pitamahah is a tatpurusha: maha (great/elder) + pita (father) with the compound meaning 'grandfather'. The second member pita is the head noun, modified by maha indicating seniority. This is a common kinship compound in Sanskrit.

episodes.source_text_iast: dasha-ahanena vyadhih prashyati iti vaidyah aha
episodes.source_text_devanagari: दशाहेन व्याधिः प्रशाम्यति इति वैद्यः आह
episodes.english_context: Charaka Samhita. The physician says the disease subsides within ten days.
episodes.decision_prompt: What type of samasa is 'dasha-ahena' (within ten days / in the span of ten days)?
candidate_options: Dvandva (copulative) — 'ten and day', two coordinate nouns
Tatpurusha (determinative) — 'day of ten', second member is head
Bahuvrihi (possessive) — 'that which has ten days', describes an outside referent
Avyayibhava (adverbial) — compound functions as an indeclinable expressing 'within ten days'
episodes.correct_answer: Avyayibhava (adverbial) — compound functions as an indeclinable expressing 'within ten days'
episodes.explanation: Dasha-ahena is avyayibhava: it functions as an indeclinable adverb meaning 'within ten days'. Avyayibhava compounds are recognized by their invariant (avyaya) behavior — they don't inflect for case — and typically express temporal, spatial, or manner relations.

episodes.english_context: Arthashastra. A compound used to describe something occurring 'throughout the entire earth'.
episodes.decision_prompt: What type of samasa is 'prithivi-upari' (above the entire earth / throughout the earth)?
candidate_options: Tatpurusha (determinative) — 'above of earth', second member is head
Avyayibhava (adverbial) — compound functions as an indeclinable adverb meaning 'above/throughout the earth'
Dvandva (copulative) — 'earth and above', two coordinate members
Bahuvrihi (possessive) — 'that which has earth above it', refers to external referent
episodes.correct_answer: Avyayibhava (adverbial) — compound functions as an indeclinable adverb meaning 'above/throughout the earth'
episodes.explanation: Prithivi-upari is avyayibhava: the first member is a noun (prithivi, earth) and the second is an indeclinable (upari, above/throughout). The whole compound functions adverbially and is itself indeclinable — a hallmark of avyayibhava.

episodes.english_context: Mahabharata, Bhishma Parva. The archer (one who bears/holds a bow) entered the battlefield.
episodes.decision_prompt: What type of samasa is 'dhanur-dharah' (the bow-bearer / archer)?
candidate_options: Tatpurusha (determinative) — 'bearer of the bow', second member is head, first is object of the action
Dvandva (copulative) — 'bow and bearer', two coordinate members
Bahuvrihi (possessive) — 'he who bears a bow', compound describes an outside person (the archer)
Avyayibhava (adverbial) — compound acts as indeclinable adverb
episodes.correct_answer: Bahuvrihi (possessive) — 'he who bears a bow', compound describes an outside person (the archer)
episodes.explanation: Dhanur-dharah is bahuvrihi: it describes an external referent (the warrior/archer) who possesses the attribute of bearing a bow. The compound itself is not about bows — it's an adjectival label for the person. Compare with tatpurusha 'dhanus-dharanam' (the act of bow-bearing) where the compound refers to the activity itself.

episodes.english_context: Vishnu Sahasranama. 'Chaturbhujah' is an epithet of Vishnu meaning 'the four-armed one'.
episodes.decision_prompt: What type of samasa is 'chaturbhujah' (the four-armed one, epithet of Vishnu)?
candidate_options: Dvandva (copulative) — 'four and arm', two coordinate members
Tatpurusha (determinative) — 'arm of four', second member is head
Bahuvrihi (possessive) — 'he who has four arms', compound refers to Vishnu as external referent
episodes.correct_answer: Bahuvrihi (possessive) — 'he who has four arms', compound refers to Vishnu as external referent
episodes.explanation: Chaturbhujah is a classic bahuvrihi: the compound means 'he who has four arms' — it describes Vishnu, who is external to the compound. The compound itself is adjectival. Key test: neither 'four' nor 'arm' is the main subject; Vishnu is.

episodes.english_context: Aryabhatiya. The sun and the moon are positioned in the sky — used in calculating their conjunction.
episodes.decision_prompt: What type of samasa is 'surya-chandramasau' (the sun and the moon)?
candidate_options: Tatpurusha (determinative) — 'moon of the sun', second member qualified by first
Dvandva (copulative) — 'sun and moon', both equally prominent, dual ending reflects two referents
Bahuvrihi (possessive) — 'he who has sun and moon', refers to an outside entity
Karmadharaya (descriptive) — adjective-noun pair, both referring to same entity
episodes.correct_answer: Dvandva (copulative) — 'sun and moon', both equally prominent, dual ending reflects two referents
episodes.explanation: Surya-chandramasau is dvandva: both members (surya, sun; chandra, moon) are coordinate and equally important semantically. The dual ending (-au) signals two referents. Dvandva is the standard compound type for pairing celestial bodies in Jyotisha texts.

episodes.english_context: Ashtanga Hridayam. The body becomes afflicted by the three doshas (vata, pitta, kapha).
episodes.decision_prompt: What type of samasa is 'tri-doshah' (the three doshas)?
candidate_options: Dvandva (copulative) — 'three and dosha', two coordinate members
Bahuvrihi (possessive) — 'that body which has three doshas', refers to external referent
Dvigu (numerical tatpurusha) — numeral + noun meaning 'a group of three doshas', subtype of tatpurusha
episodes.correct_answer: Dvigu (numerical tatpurusha) — numeral + noun meaning 'a group of three doshas', subtype of tatpurusha
episodes.explanation: Tri-doshah is a dvigu compound: a numeral (tri, three) + noun (dosha) forming a collective meaning 'the group of three doshas'. Dvigu is a subtype of tatpurusha where the first member is always a numeral. It typically takes singular or neuter endings to signal collectivity.

episodes.source_text_iast: sat-citam ananda-rupam brahma iti vedah vadanti
episodes.english_context: Mandukya Upanishad. The Vedas declare Brahman to be sat-cit-ananda (existence-consciousness-bliss).
episodes.decision_prompt: What type of samasa is 'sat-cit-ananda' (existence, consciousness, bliss — the three aspects of Brahman)?
candidate_options: Tatpurusha (determinative) — 'bliss of consciousness of being', chain of qualifications
Dvandva (copulative) — 'existence and consciousness and bliss', three coordinate equal members
Bahuvrihi (possessive) — 'that which has existence, consciousness and bliss', refers to Brahman externally
Avyayibhava (adverbial) — functions as indeclinable in Vedantic discourse
episodes.correct_answer: Dvandva (copulative) — 'existence and consciousness and bliss', three coordinate equal members
episodes.explanation: Sat-cit-ananda is a dvandva: all three members (sat, cit, ananda) are semantically equal and coordinate — 'being AND consciousness AND bliss'. Dvandva can have more than two members. The compound serves as a technical definition of Brahman's nature in Advaita Vedanta.

episodes.english_context: Mahabharata. The warriors saw the lotus-eyed one (Vishnu/Krishna). 'Padma-nayanam' is a common epithet.
episodes.decision_prompt: What type of samasa is 'padma-nayanam' (lotus-eyed / he whose eyes are like lotuses)?
candidate_options: Karmadharaya (descriptive tatpurusha) — 'lotus eye', adjective + noun referring to same entity
Dvandva (copulative) — 'lotus and eye', two coordinate members
Bahuvrihi (possessive) — 'he whose eyes are like lotuses', compound refers to the deity as external referent
Tatpurusha (determinative) — 'eye of the lotus', second member is head qualified by first
episodes.correct_answer: Bahuvrihi (possessive) — 'he whose eyes are like lotuses', compound refers to the deity as external referent
episodes.explanation: Padma-nayanam is bahuvrihi: it is an adjectival epithet describing an external referent (Vishnu/Krishna) whose eyes resemble lotuses. The compound itself doesn't mean 'lotus' or 'eye' — it means 'the one possessing lotus-like eyes'. This is the most common use of bahuvrihi in Sanskrit poetry for divine epithets.

episodes.english_context: Aryabhatiya. The astronomer calculates the daily motion of the planets.
episodes.decision_prompt: What type of samasa is 'pratyaham' (every day / daily)?
candidate_options: Tatpurusha (determinative) — 'day of prati', second member is head
Dvandva (copulative) — 'prati and day', two coordinate members
Avyayibhava (adverbial) — indeclinable compound meaning 'every day', first member is indeclinable prefix 'prati'
Bahuvrihi (possessive) — 'that which has every day', refers to external referent
episodes.correct_answer: Avyayibhava (adverbial) — indeclinable compound meaning 'every day', first member is indeclinable prefix 'prati'
episodes.explanation: Pratyaham is a canonical avyayibhava: prati (a prefix/indeclinable meaning 'each/every') + aham (day). The entire compound functions as an adverb ('daily') and is itself indeclinable. Prati-prefixed avyayibhava compounds (pratidinam, pratyabdam, etc.) are extremely common in technical Sanskrit literature.

episodes.english_context: Upanishad passage. The teacher and student walk together on the path of dharma.
episodes.decision_prompt: What type of samasa is 'guru-shishyau' (teacher and student)?
candidate_options: Tatpurusha (determinative) — 'student of the teacher', second member is head qualified by first
Dvandva (copulative) — 'teacher and student', both members equally prominent, dual confirms two referents
Bahuvrihi (possessive) — 'he who has teacher and student', refers to external entity
Karmadharaya (descriptive) — adjective-noun pair referring to the same entity
episodes.correct_answer: Dvandva (copulative) — 'teacher and student', both members equally prominent, dual confirms two referents
episodes.explanation: Guru-shishyau is dvandva: both members (guru, teacher; shishya, student) are semantically coordinate — 'teacher AND student'. The dual ending (-au) confirms exactly two referents. This is identical in structure to rama-lakshmanau (t4_003).

episodes.english_context: Synthetic samasa classification example #1 in astronomy.
episodes.decision_prompt: What is the samasa type of 'ati-putra' in this usage?
episodes.explanation: This example is labeled as Karmadharaya (descriptive apposition) based on head-modifier semantics in this constructed context.
